title: Starting to Play Hockey 101: The Complete Beginner's Guide
blurb: The whole journey from never-laced-up to your first stride, in five simple steps.

body.text: Every player who's ever stepped onto the ice started exactly where you are now: a little nervous, a lot excited, and unsure what they actually need. Good news: hockey is far more welcoming than it looks from the stands. This guide walks you through the five things that get you from the sidelines to your first stride.
Take it one section at a time. You don't need to master everything before you show up. You just need to start.
1. Get comfortable on the ice

Before sticks and pucks, there's skating. If you can stand up, glide, and stop, you're already playing hockey. Everything else builds on those three things. Spend a few public-skate sessions just getting your balance before you worry about anything fancy.
Keep your knees bent, your weight slightly forward, and your head up. Falling is part of it for everyone, so wear gloves and don't be shy about grabbing the boards early on.
Book a Learn-to-Skate session at your local rink. An hour with a coach beats a month of guessing, and most rinks run beginner blocks on weekends.
2. Gather the essential gear

Hockey gear looks intimidating (and expensive), but beginners need far less than a fully-kitted vet. Start with the safety basics: a helmet, skates that fit, and gloves. You can borrow, rent, or buy used for almost everything else while you figure out whether the sport sticks.

body.title: Build your first kit — and see what to skip
body.text: Compare beginner-friendly gear options, prices, and rent-vs-buy calls on our Equipment page.
3. Learn the basic rules

You don't need the whole rulebook — just enough to not feel lost. Learn what offsides and icing mean, how a shift works, and why players keep swapping on the fly. The rest you'll pick up naturally by watching and playing.
At the beginner level, referees and teammates are patient. Ask questions on the bench. Nobody expects a first-timer to know every whistle.
4. Find beginner-friendly ice

Where you play matters as much as how. Look for drop-in sessions labeled “beginner” or “learn-to-play,” adult learn-to-play leagues, and rinks that run intro clinics. These are full of people exactly like you — not weekend warriors who'll skate circles around a newcomer.
Not all “open hockey” is beginner-friendly — some sessions are competitive. Check the skill level in the listing, or call the rink, before you show up.
5. Just start playing

This is the one that matters most. You will not feel “ready.” Nobody does. Sign up for that beginner session, lace up, and get out there. Every rep on the ice teaches you more than another video ever will, and the hockey community is famously happy to welcome new players.
Show up, have fun, and give yourself permission to be new. That's the whole secret.

title: Hockey Gear Checklist: Everything You Need (and What You Can Skip)
blurb: What's required, what's worth adding later, and what beginners can safely skip.
tocIntroLabel: What gear do I actually need?

body.text: Every new player asks the same question before their first session: what do I actually need to bring? The list looks long at first, but most of it is safety gear you'll only buy once. Here's what's required, what's worth adding once you're playing regularly, and what you can skip for now.
One note before the checklist: requirements vary by league and age group, so double-check anything below with your rink or coach.
1. Get the must-have gear
These are the pieces nearly every player needs before stepping onto the ice.
Protective equipment:

items: Full face cage or visor (age- and league-dependent)

body.text: Skates and stick:
Base layers:

body.title: Compare skates, helmets, and pads by price
body.text: See real specs and retailer pricing for beginner-friendly gear on our Equipment page.
2. Consider the nice-to-haves
None of these are required, but they're worth adding once you're playing regularly.

items: Skate soakers for protecting blades after skating
Hard skate guards for walking around the rink

body.text: 3. Do a two-minute bag check
A quick check before you leave can save you from showing up short a glove or a skate.

items: Full set of pads in your bag

body.text: Keep this list on your phone or taped inside your bag. It takes two minutes and beats driving back home for a forgotten skate.
4. Skip these for now
It's easy to feel like you need every gadget and premium upgrade when you're starting out. Most beginners can safely skip:

items: Premium accessories designed for elite-level players
Fancy stick waxes and specialty grip products
High-end equipment bags loaded with extra features

body.text: Don't skimp on fit to save money. Put your budget toward gear that fits properly, especially your helmet and skates. A well-fitted setup improves your game far more than the priciest model on the shelf.
5. Get properly fitted

If you're buying gear for the first time, visit a local hockey shop and get fitted in person. Many shops also carry quality used equipment, so you can get started without overspending. After a season or two, you'll have a much better sense of where upgrading actually makes a difference.

title: The Basic Rules of Hockey Explained
blurb: Just enough of the rulebook to stop feeling lost on the ice or in the stands.

body.text: Hockey moves fast and play stops for a lot of different reasons, which is exactly why it can look confusing to a new fan or player. In reality, a handful of core rules cover almost everything you'll see in a game.
This guide covers the basics. For the two rules beginners get tripped up on most, offside/icing and penalties, we've got dedicated guides linked below.
1. The objective and the basics
The goal is simple: score more than the other team. A goal counts once the puck fully crosses the goal line between the posts and under the crossbar.

Each team plays six skaters at a time: one goaltender, two defensemen, and three forwards (center, left wing, right wing). Games run three periods, with length varying by league, and can end in a tie, overtime, or a shootout depending on where you're playing.
2. The rink and faceoffs
The rink splits into three zones:

items: Defensive zone: contains your team's net
Neutral zone: between the two blue lines
Offensive zone: contains the opposing team's net

body.text: A faceoff, two players lining up while an official drops the puck, starts or restarts play after most stoppages: the start of each period, and after goals, penalties, and rule violations.
Watching a game for the first time? Keep an eye on the blue lines and the referee's arm signals. Most stoppages make a lot more sense once you can spot them coming.
3. Offside and icing

These are the two rules that trip up almost every newcomer. Offside means an attacking player entered the offensive zone before the puck did. Icing means shooting the puck the length of the ice from your own side without anyone touching it, which stops play and sends the faceoff back into your own zone.
Both have real nuance worth its own read (delayed offside, when icing gets waived off) — see our offside and icing guide below.
4. Penalties and power plays

A penalty is called when a player breaks a safety or fair-play rule. Tripping, hooking, holding, slashing, and interference are the common ones. Minor penalties usually cost two minutes in the box, majors cost five, and serious misconduct can end a player's night entirely.

While a player serves a penalty, their team plays shorthanded and the other team is on the power play, usually the best scoring chance in the game. See our penalties guide below for the full list of common calls.

Body-checking rules vary a lot by league, age group, and level. Many beginner and recreational leagues don't allow intentional checking at all, so review your specific league's contact rules before your first game.
5. Line changes and stoppages

Players substitute while play continues, called changing on the fly. Jump on too early and your team gets called for too many players. Shifts stay short on purpose: tired legs skate slower and make worse decisions.
If you're brand new, focus on these first:

items: The puck must enter the zone before you do (offside)
Don't shoot the puck the length of the ice out of pressure (icing)
Avoid tripping, holding, hooking, slashing, or interfering

body.text: You don't need the whole rulebook to enjoy or play hockey. Get comfortable with goals, offside, icing, penalties, and line changes, and the rest comes naturally by watching and playing.

title: Essential Hockey Skills Every Beginner Should Learn First
blurb: The handful of skating and stick skills worth practicing before anything else.

body.text: Hockey asks a lot at once: balance on skates, puck control, positioning, and split-second decisions, all while everyone around you is moving. The good news is you don't have to master all of it before your first game. Build these skills roughly in order, and the rest gets easier.
Skating comes first. Every other skill on this list gets easier once you can move, stop, and turn with control.
A practical order to learn them in:
1. Balance, stance, and falling

Start with a stable hockey stance: knees bent, feet about shoulder-width apart, chest and head up, weight centered over your skates. Standing too upright makes it harder to accelerate, turn, and recover your balance.

Falling is part of learning, for everyone. Practice it: bend your knees instead of locking them and let your pads absorb the impact, then get back up by rolling onto your knees, planting one skate flat, and pushing up.
Spend a few minutes each session just gliding in your stance with no puck, staying low and looking ahead instead of down at the ice.
2. Forward skating, stopping, and turning
Efficient strides come from pushing outward and backward through the inside edge of your skate, not short choppy steps. Aim for one strong push followed by a controlled glide.

For stopping, start with a snowplow: bend your knees, turn your toes slightly inward, and push your heels outward to create friction. A hockey stop, turning both skates sideways to shave the ice, comes once that feels natural. Practice it in both directions.
Turning follows the same rule as everything else here: look where you want to go. Beginners who stare at their skates lose balance or turn too late.
3. Backward skating

It feels unnatural at first, but every position uses it, not just defense. Most players start with C-cuts: knees bent, toes slightly inward, pushing one skate outward in a curved motion before bringing it back underneath you. The movement comes from your legs and edges, not from swinging your upper body.
4. Puck skills: stickhandling, passing, and shooting
You don't need fast hands right away, just control. Keep the puck close but not directly between your skates, and practice moving it from forehand to backhand slowly before adding speed.

A good beginner pass doesn't need to be hard. It needs to be accurate, flat along the ice, and easy for a teammate to receive. The same goes for shooting: work on the wrist shot and aim for the whole net before chasing corners. Slap shots can wait.
Gripping the stick too tightly is the most common beginner habit to break. A relaxed grip makes stickhandling, passing, and shooting all smoother.
5. Positioning and awareness

A beginner doesn't need to memorize systems, but a few habits help immediately: spread out instead of crowding the puck, stay between the puck and your own net when the other team has it, and scan the ice before a pass arrives so you already know your next move.
Simple calls like “here” or “time” help teammates make faster decisions, too. You don't need to shout constantly, just be clear when it counts.

These skills overlap, so you don't need to master them one at a time; a single session might mix skating, passing, and shooting. But skating stays the priority. Better skating gives you more time, balance, and control in almost every situation on the ice.

title: Common Beginner Hockey Mistakes (and How to Avoid Them)
blurb: The little habits that trip up new players, and the easy fixes for each.
tocIntroLabel: What mistakes should I watch for?

body.text: Every new player makes mistakes. Hockey asks you to skate, handle the puck, watch everyone else move, and make quick decisions, all at the same time. The goal isn't to avoid every mistake right away. It's to catch the habits that slow you down and swap them for better ones.
Here are the ones that show up most often, and how to fix them.
1. Skating habits

items: Standing too upright: bend your knees and keep your weight centered over your skates. It's easier to balance, turn, and stop from a lower stance.
Short, choppy strides: push fully through one skate before bringing it back underneath you, then glide. Longer strides are faster with less effort.
Only practicing your strong side: stop, turn, and pass in both directions. The weaker side feels awkward at first but catches up with repetition.

body.text: 2. Puck-handling habits

items: Staring at the puck: practice moving it slowly while looking up. You'll still glance down when you need to, just not constantly.
Gripping the stick too tightly: keep your hands relaxed, especially the bottom hand. A stiff grip makes every puck skill harder than it needs to be.
Holding the puck too long: decide what you'll do before it arrives. A simple pass or safe clear usually beats waiting for the perfect play.

body.text: 3. On-ice decisions

items: Chasing the puck: not every player needs to join the battle. Look for open space or an uncovered teammate instead.
Standing still after a pass: keep moving into open space so you stay a passing option.
Reaching with the stick instead of skating: work your feet to get back into position. Reaching leads to tripping and hooking calls.

body.text: Trying advanced moves, like crossovers, dekes, or slap shots, before your fundamentals are solid usually reinforces bad habits instead of fixing them. Build balance and edge control first.
4. Shifts and communication

items: Shifts that run too long: change when the puck is in a safe area, and skate hard while you're out there. Short, active shifts beat long, tired ones.
Changing at the wrong time: avoid changing while the other team is attacking toward your net.
Staying quiet: simple calls like “here,” “time,” or a teammate's name help everyone make faster decisions.

body.text: 5. Equipment and mindset
Gear that doesn't fit well affects comfort, movement, and confidence. Choose skates and a helmet based on fit, not price or looks.
And don't measure your progress against other players. Everyone starts hockey with a different skating background and a different amount of practice time. Consistency matters more than speed.
Game reps add up slower than you'd think. A few focused minutes of off-ice stickhandling or a stick-and-puck session can do more for a specific skill than an entire game.
None of this needs to click at once. Stay low, keep your head up, make simple plays, and work on one habit at a time. Every experienced player made these same mistakes first.
